paragraphs.body: You think you know everything at 17. I thought I was so put together at 17 and had my life figured out. Little did I know I’m the span of a year everything that I thought that I knew would be thrown out the window! At 17 I had my whole life planned out. I had a goal I set my mind to it and I knew what I needed to do to accomplish that goal: I knew I needed good grades, so I did that, I knew I needed to make varsity on my sport all 4 years, I did that. I knew I needed to make all-state all. 4 years and I accomplished that. I put countless hours, blood, sweat and tears into this sport. But it didn’t even feel like work because I enjoyed it so much. As a senior in high school, at 17 years old. I had what a lot of teenagers only dream of. My choice to go to absolutely any school I wanted to. I didn’t realize how much of a blessing that was at the time. Before my first visit at Pitt, so from ages 14-16 I always said Pitt was my last choice, and if I went to Pitt than it was a “cry for help!” But I was proved wrong and taken back. I went on my visit and I think the glitz and glimmer caught up to me and blinded me. I saw a nice apartment and big flashy power 4 athlete perks and I was sold.

annotations.text: I saw a nice apartment and big flashy power 4 athlete perks and I was sold
annotations.comment: I wonder if you could explore some of the places like this in your essay. I am curious what drew you to the apartment. I would love to know more what you mean by perks of a power 4 athlete--what specific perks attracted you the most?

paragraphs.body: I was blinded by the flashy blue and yellow colors. This was such a high for me at 17 I genuinely felt on top of the world. When I came back to school after my visit. It genuinely felt like all my hard work paid off. Everyone thought it was so cool that not only was I going to the University of Pittsburgh, I was going to be an athlete there. Now some might say this causes a lot of outside noise, and pressure but to be honest nobody puts more pressure on me than myself. It was so motivating to work hard because I knew I had to be good to be running track at the university of Pittsburgh. At the end of my senior year of high school I learned the important lesson of knowing the importance of being confident in my running. The summer before my freshman year is crucial for training so I can come in and prove my spot on the team. Now we’re going to flash forward to November. Things were going well in practice and I was so exited for our upcoming meet. Truth be told I was more exited for this than Christmas, my training was going well and things were looking good for me. December 6th little did I know this would be my last time running in a meet until January of 2026. Because I was a freshman who had no recorded college time, I had to run in a pre prelim. I had the 2nd fastest time in the meet. The only girl ahead of me was a jr on our team. I ran the semi finals. I got in 8/8 the only freshman in this final. I knew when we were all lined up and that gun went off placement ment nothing. We were all starting at the same line and I had the same chance to finish first just as much as anyone else in that race.unfortunatly my strained groin got in the way and I finished 6th with my worst time of the day. Although I was not happy with that performance at all, I was willing to take it because it was my first college meet a

annotations.text: I had the 2nd fastest time in the meet. The only girl ahead of me was a jr on our team. I ran the semi finals. I got in 8/8 the only freshman in this final. I knew when we were all lined up and that gun went off placement ment nothing. We were all starting at the same line and I had the same chance to finish first just as much as anyone else in that race.unfortunatly my strained groin got in the way and I finished 6th with my worst time of the da
annotations.comment: This is my favorite passage in this essay--here you are moving well beyond generalizations and fousing on the specific details of that one meet. It really places your reader in the moment.

paragraphs.body: I was slightly hurt and I knew it could go up from there. Little did I know at my next practice they would make me a thrower. None the less I tricked on and really tried to make the most of my new role on the team.flash forward to the end of February. My mental health is going down the drain I know this place is not for me and I do t have to stay in this situation. I tell the coaches that I am transferring. He says “you’ll never find somewhere as good as here, your making a mistake, you want to be a big fish in a small pound and your not as competitive as I thought. ” You think things can’t get any worse… right? My throwing coach thought it would be fun to gossip with some of my teammates “ she’s a trophy kid, who’s parents tell her she’s better than she is, she is never going to be able to go to another power 4 school she’s lucky if she even stays d1, she’s an ungrateful brat who has never been through something hard. ” That’s a big claim for an 18 year old girl who is just doing what’s best for her mental health. Also they don’t know even a quarter of my story and why track means so much to me and everything I’ve been through.

annotations.comment: Hello, The central controlling idea of this piece seems to be about how one’s hopes and dreams can be stolen away from them by an unjust system. The big ideas that support this develop through the hard work you put into attaining your goals for several years, your relative success in your first meet, and your coaches putting you into a role that you never wanted, expected, or trained for. The single phrase that stood out to me the most is power 4 athlete. This is elite. You are an elite athlete. You always will be. This phrase stands out because there are so few people in the world to whom this word refers. You can compete with the best of the best of the best runners in the country. Your coaches can’t take that away from you. You know that even if they don’t, and you will find a school that is a better fit for you, because you are elite. The thing that I admire most about this essay is the narrative. You begin the piece several years ago as you were beginning to make your decisions about college, and you bring your reader through the years to the moment of your first collegiate race and the devastating events that follow. I think the central audience of this piece is potential incoming student athletes: in a sense it’s you from two years ago. If you had read this story before committing, you might have seriously reconsidered coming to Pitt at all. I imagine before the day it happened, you never even knew it was a possibility that the coaches could or would want to do this to you. If you could reach out to high school students who could be affected by similar circumstances, you might help them make a very difficult decision. The thing that I learned about this, or, at least, the thing that this essay reinforced for me is this: while the events of the essay are clearly important to you, this is not the end of your story. This is not even the end of this chapter of your story. Unless you allow it to be. If you allow your coaches’ behaviors and decisions to dictate how your story is told, this could be a sad ending. On the other hand, if you face this challenge as you have faced all of your challenges in the past, you can recognize that this is one moment in your own personal heroic journey that you will rise above. And, after you’ve risen, you can look back at this unfair treatment as the challenge that you conquered that ultimately is going to make you an even more powerful human being than you were before. I’ve thought about your story often, and, for what it’s worth, it was a pleasure reading it here in full. I, for one, am looking forward to seeing how you rise above this current difficulty and choose to end this chapter for yourself. Thanks for sharing,
